policy_statement: Create a performance framework and educational programming on low-carbon materials best practices for industry professionals and the public, focusing on low-income communities and communities of color.
mechanism_description: Providing performance guidance and educational programming increases adoption of low-carbon construction materials and practices, reducing lifecycle (embodied) greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Guidance/education → material & practice substitution → ↓ industrial/process energy use + ↓ process emissions (embodied carbon) → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Building owners, designers, contractors, and suppliers shift procurement/specification decisions toward low-carbon materials (e.g., low-clinker cement, SCM blends, EPD-informed procurement, mass timber where appropriate, recycled-content materials) and improved construction practices due to performance frameworks, training, and outreach.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Lower-carbon material production pathways and reduced material intensity decrease fossil fuel combustion for heat/electricity and reduce process emissions (notably calcination in cement), plus potentially reduce upstream extraction/transport burdens.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in embodied/lifecycle GHG emissions associated with construction materials and projects.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced lifecycle (embodied) GHG emissions from producing and supplying construction materials due to increased adoption of lower-carbon alternatives.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Education/performance guidance changes purchasing/specification behavior → adoption of low-carbon materials/practices → lower embodied emissions in material supply chains → reduced GHG emissions (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can improve resource efficiency (using less material or more recycled content), the mechanism is explicitly framed around reducing lifecycle/embodied GHG emissions; the climate-relevant endpoint is emissions reduction rather than primarily reduced input use.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a program emphasized material efficiency only (same service with less material/energy) it could lean Resource Efficiency, but under this canonical mechanism the intended and typical dominant effect is embodied emissions mitigation. It is not primarily Adaptation (no hazard/exposure pathway) and not Nature-Based Solutions (no ecosystem sink pathway).  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy using “education/performance guidance to increase low-carbon materials adoption” will primarily act through reduced upstream/process emissions from materials, yielding mitigation benefits regardless of city/context.  
5) Secondary subtype deferral: Any secondary tagging (e.g., cement decarbonization vs. timber substitution vs. procurement practice change; equity/community co-benefits) must be assigned at the individual policy level based on the specific program design and targeted materials.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If “low-carbon materials” are achieved mainly via material minimization/reuse without clear GHG accounting, the primary could be arguable as Resource Efficiency; however, the stated mechanism centers on embodied GHG reduction, making Mitigation the consistent primary category.
key_indicators: “Educate stakeholders”; “Create a performance framework and educational programming”; “materials best practices”; “reduce the lifecycle… impacts of building materials and construction practices”; “industry professionals and the general public”; “low-income communities and communities of color”
co_benefits: Reduced negative health impacts from building materials/construction practices; equity and targeted capacity-building for low-income communities and communities of color
instance_edge_case_notes: “Performance framework” could be interpreted as a voluntary standard/benchmark (performance-standard), but the text frames it as part of education/programming rather than a mandated requirement; classified as information-disclosure given the dominant mechanism is guidance/training.
